title: What EfficientNet Taught Model Designers
concept: EfficientNet taught model designers that scaling is itself a design problem: instead of treating size increases as an afterthought, they can plan how architecture, budget, and performance should grow together — a bridge toward ideas like neural architecture search.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what is the best mental model for EfficientNet-B0, B1, B2, and similar names?

options.text: They are scaled members of one family built from a shared core design
They are unrelated models that only share a letter
They are training datasets, not CNN architectures

exercises.explain: Correct — that family view leads to EfficientNet's deeper design lesson.

cards.title: Scaling stopped being an afterthought
cards.body: Before ideas like EfficientNet, it was easy to treat scaling as something you did later: take a model, then make it deeper or wider if you need more power. EfficientNet pushed a more deliberate view: how a model grows should be part of the design from the start.

cards.title: What lesson did designers take away?
cards.body: EfficientNet's big lesson is that scaling strategy is itself a design decision. Model quality is not only about the base block or layer type; it is also about how that base architecture expands across depth, width, and resolution as budget changes.

cards.title: Design the expansion plan with the building
cards.body: When architects design a campus, they often plan not just the first building but how future wings can be added without breaking the layout. EfficientNet treats model design similarly: the growth plan belongs in the blueprint, not only in last-minute expansion.

cards.title: Choosing a backbone becomes more systematic
cards.body: Suppose a team needs one vision backbone for experiments today and a larger version later for production. EfficientNet suggests they should not ask only 'Which model is best right now?' but also 'How does this design scale when our budget changes?' That thinking influences later model families and search methods.

cards.title: Scaling is not just a cleanup step
cards.body: A common misconception is that scaling happens after the 'real' architecture work is done. EfficientNet challenged that idea. In practice, the scaling rule can shape whether a model family remains practical across phones, laptops, and servers.

cards.title: Before and after EfficientNet thinking
cards.body: The shift was conceptual as much as architectural.

left.text:
Pick a model first
Grow one dimension later
Hope the larger version still makes sense

right.text:
Plan how the family should grow
Coordinate size with compute budget
Use scaling rules intentionally

exercises.prompt: What broad lesson did EfficientNet teach beyond its own specific model family?

options.text: How a model scales should be treated as part of architecture design, not as a random later tweak
All future CNNs must use the exact EfficientNet name to work
Model scaling should ignore hardware budgets entirely
Depth, width, and resolution are old-fashioned and never matter now

exercises.explain: Exactly — the design lesson is larger than one brand of model.

exercises.prompt: True or false: EfficientNet helped encourage the idea that model families should be designed with growth rules, not just a single frozen model size.
exercises.explain: True — it made scaling itself feel more systematic and intentional.

exercises.sentence: EfficientNet suggested that scaling should be treated as a design ___, not an afterthought.
exercises.explain: Right — the architecture must include a thoughtful growth plan.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best bridges EfficientNet into neural architecture search (NAS)?

options.text: If scaling rules matter so much, then designers may also ask whether search methods can help discover strong base architectures and scaling choices
NAS exists only because CNNs stopped using images
EfficientNet proved no more architecture design questions remained

exercises.explain: Yes — once scaling becomes a design question, it naturally connects to methods that search for good designs.

exercises.prompt: Why is EfficientNet a bridge into neural architecture search (NAS)?

options.text: Because it showed that architecture quality includes not just the base network, but also how that network should be scaled across budgets — which opens the door to searching for better designs and scaling rules
Because NAS is only a new file-naming system for EfficientNet variants
Because EfficientNet proved architecture choices no longer matter once training starts
Because NAS exists only to make models deeper and ignore all other axes

exercises.explain: Exactly. EfficientNet taught that scaling is part of architecture design itself. Next: neural architecture search, where researchers ask whether algorithms can help discover strong model structures and scaling choices automatically.
